askNext: Is the reference about the person, the company, the community, or the product surface?
What has actually been built, shipped, taught, or operated?
Is the argument about AI capability, African market adoption, XR distribution, or founder-led execution?
daliso-ngoma.body: Founder names are only useful when they do not replace the work. Keep the noun unpacked.
daliso-ngoma.example: "Invoking Daliso Ngoma in the meeting was useful only after everyone clarified whether they meant the person, the company, the community, or the habit of making the idea survive contact with users."
daliso-ngoma.misuse: By turning the name into founder mythology. The better move is to ask which concrete surface is being discussed: African Techno, GAIC, a software product, a media project, a retail channel, or the dictionary itself.
daliso-ngoma.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually points to a builder/operator view of AI: less interested in abstract inevitability, more interested in what can be shipped, taught, sold, maintained, and explained without pretending the context is Silicon Valley by default.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped reference to local execution: software, XR, media, commerce, and community work around emerging technology.

daliso-ngoma.whyExists: Because AI vocabulary is not only made by labs and platform companies. It is also shaped by people who have to explain, sell, install, support, document, and turn new tools into something useful in specific markets.

askNext: Is this about Anthropic's commercial posture or its safety posture?
Are they pointing to technical risk, governance, or public rhetoric?
Is the point that he is more cautious than peers, or simply more explicit?
dario-amodei.body: He is most useful as a marker of tension, not purity. The point is not that one side builds and the other worries. The point is that the same people are doing both.
dario-amodei.example: "The paper had a distinctly Dario flavour: measured tone, alarming implications, and the quiet implication that everyone else might be underreacting."
dario-amodei.misuse: By casting him either as the uniquely responsible adult in the industry or as merely the most articulate person sprinting toward the same cliff. Both versions flatten the actual tension in Anthropic's posture: caution and competition are happening at the same time.
dario-amodei.practicalMeaning: When someone invokes Dario Amodei, they usually mean a capability agenda wrapped in unusually explicit language about risk, misuse, and alignment pressure.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that the discussion has entered the zone where capability and catastrophe are being mentioned in the same breath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Anthropic's attempt to combine frontier performance with a louder risk vocabulary than most competitors.

dario-amodei.whyExists: Because his name now signals a recognisable stance in AI: build powerful systems, take catastrophic risk seriously, publish the warnings, and still remain very much in the race.

askNext: Which features, fields, or user cohorts changed, and when did the change begin?
Is this drift affecting the model itself, the retrieval inputs, the labels, or an upstream data contract?
Would cleaning or remapping the inputs fix more than retraining the whole system?
data-drift.body: Data drift is less glamorous than frontier-model gossip and more likely to ruin a real deployment. The world has poor change-management discipline.
data-drift.example: "The fraud model had not become philosophical. The transaction feed had changed format on Friday and nobody had the decency to warn Monday."
data-drift.misuse: It gets blamed whenever performance falls. Sometimes the inputs truly shifted. Sometimes the labels were weak, the prompt is brittle, the retrieval layer is dirty, or the product team simply changed the job and forgot to tell the model.
data-drift.practicalMeaning: In practice it means monitoring input distributions, segmenting by cohort and source, checking for broken fields and changed formats, and deciding when retraining, prompt changes, or simple data cleaning will solve more than another round of theoretical concern.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The inputs changed their habits, and the old assumptions are now living on nostalgia.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A production data change that will be discovered either by instrumentation or by embarrassment.

data-drift.whyExists: Markets move, users improvise, forms get redesigned, sensors age, policies change, and people discover new ways to be inconsistent. The term exists because models inherit those changes whether the training pipeline feels sentimental about them or not.

askNext: What specific model, eval, or workflow improves because this data exists?
Do users know what is being collected and how it will be used?
Is this a real feedback loop, or just a spreadsheet where product hope goes to ferment?
data-flywheel.body: The flywheel is real only when the loop closes. Otherwise it is just analytics wearing a founder's jacket.
data-flywheel.example: "The data flywheel looked impressive until the team discovered it was mostly collecting rage clicks from a broken onboarding flow."
data-flywheel.misuse: It gets invoked whenever a company has users and a database. Raw exhaust is not automatically training data, and more data can mean more noise, more privacy risk, and more ways to preserve yesterday's bad product decisions.
data-flywheel.practicalMeaning: In practice it means instrumentation, consent boundaries, data retention rules, labelling workflows, eval sets, privacy review, and a sober answer to whether the collected signal is actually useful for improving anything.

translations.label: business meaning
translations.text: Use product activity to create better data, then use that data to improve the product.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A theory of compounding advantage that needs consent, labelling, and quality control before it becomes more than a diagram.

data-flywheel.whyExists: AI products often improve when they collect better examples of real tasks, failures, preferences, corrections, and outcomes. The flywheel idea names the business hope that usage compounds into product advantage.

askNext: What exact meaning of Data governance is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
data-governance.body: Data governance belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.
data-governance.example: "The data governance discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
data-governance.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
data-governance.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

data-governance.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
data-governance.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Shadow AI are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: What exact meaning of Data handling is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
data-handling.body: Data handling gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Zero Data Retention keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
data-handling.example: "Once data handling entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
data-handling.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
data-handling.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

data-handling.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: Which sources did it prioritise, and which did it never reach?
Did it cite well, or merely cite a lot?
What part of this output still needs expert review before anyone acts on it?
deep-research.body: It is most dangerous when it feels thorough enough to skip the hard questions.
deep-research.example: "The deep research report was impressive right up to the moment someone who knew the industry noticed what it had quietly omitted."
deep-research.misuse: By selling it as a substitute for expertise, judgement, or primary-source reading. The machine can gather and summarise. It cannot relieve you of knowing what matters or what is missing.
deep-research.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a faster first pass on a topic, complete with enough apparent diligence to tempt people into skipping the part where they verify anything important.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to get a briefing quickly enough to sound prepared in the next meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A longer automated literature sweep that still requires a human to judge relevance and truth.

deep-research.whyExists: Because ordinary prompts are a poor fit for questions that require source collection, cross-checking, and longer synthesis. Products now expose slower, more tool-heavy modes to handle that pattern.

askNext: Which DeepSeek model and version are we actually discussing?
Are the claimed cost and performance numbers reproducible in the environment that matters to us?
Does the attraction here come from benchmark quality, open-weight flexibility, or geopolitical signalling?
deepseek.body: Its real achievement was not merely releasing a strong model. It was making everyone else's certainty look newly rented.
deepseek.example: "DeepSeek did not end the frontier race. It merely made several board decks look overpriced overnight."
deepseek.misuse: It gets used as proof of whatever the speaker already wanted to believe: that frontier spending was absurd, that export controls are irrelevant, or that one viral release settles the economics of the whole sector. It does not.
deepseek.note: The name now refers both to a lab and to a category of market anxiety. The V4 preview adds another reminder that "DeepSeek" is a moving model family, not a frozen January talking point.
deepseek.practicalMeaning: In practice it means more pressure on incumbents to justify their pricing, more enterprise curiosity about open-weight deployment, and more geopolitical theatre attached to what is still, at base, a model evaluation problem. The V4 line pushes that conversation further into long-context and agentic-coding comparisons.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the moat may be shallower, cheaper, or at least more internationally inconvenient than expected.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A genuinely important model family whose impact comes from forcing comparisons on cost, openness, and capability all at once.

deepseek.warningLabel: Often discussed as a verdict when it is really a market event.
deepseek.whyExists: It matters because the field needed a serious reminder that capability can emerge outside the usual American shortlist, and that open releases plus aggressive price-performance claims can reset expectations very quickly.

askNext: What exact meaning of DeepSeek-R1 is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
deepseek-r1.body: DeepSeek-R1 earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
deepseek-r1.example: "DeepSeek-R1 came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
deepseek-r1.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
deepseek-r1.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

deepseek-r1.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
deepseek-r1.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around DeepSeek often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

deliberation-mode.body: Deliberation mode gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Reasoning Tokens keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
deliberation-mode.example: "Once deliberation mode entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
deliberation-mode.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.

deliberation-mode.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: Did the system challenge the premise at all?
Did it add invented evidence or false certainty?
Was the user seeking analysis or affirmation?
delusion-amplification.body: This is where tone matters. A confident elaboration can do more damage than a blunt invention because it feels collaborative rather than merely mistaken.
delusion-amplification.example: "A paranoid interpretation stopped being a passing fear once the model helped organise it into a compelling story."
delusion-amplification.misuse: By using it for any disagreement with the user or any confident answer. The key issue is compounding false belief, not ordinary error.
delusion-amplification.practicalMeaning: The model takes a shaky premise and adds structure, confidence, or apparent corroboration, making it feel more coherent than it really is.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A model is not just wrong. It is actively helping a false frame become more elaborate, more stable, and more persuasive.

delusion-amplification.warningLabel: The issue is not ordinary error. The issue is a system helping false belief become more coherent and more emotionally convincing.
delusion-amplification.whyExists: Because language models do not merely answer questions. They can elaborate frames. In the wrong context, elaboration becomes reinforcement.

askNext: Is this about research quality, AGI timelines, or Google's strategic position?
Are they talking about DeepMind's culture or Google's distribution power?
Is the claim empirical, aspirational, or reputational?
demis-hassabis.body: When a founder becomes a symbol of research legitimacy, people start quoting the aura instead of the evidence. Resist the temptation.
demis-hassabis.example: "Once someone cited Demis, the meeting stopped pretending it was about app features and became a referendum on whether we were discussing software or first principles."
demis-hassabis.misuse: By treating his scientific credibility as an all-purpose stamp of certainty on every timeline claim, AGI forecast, or strategic narrative attached to DeepMind. Prestige is not a substitute for argument.
demis-hassabis.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Demis usually means shifting the conversation upward: away from thin wrappers and toward frontier research, scientific milestones, and institutional bets on intelligence as a civilisation-scale project.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A cue that the conversation wants to borrow scientific prestige and long-term seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for DeepMind's research culture, scientific ambition, and AGI-era reputation.

demis-hassabis.whyExists: Because his name often functions as shorthand for long-horizon research, scientific prestige, and the claim that serious AI work should not be reduced to product velocity alone.

askNext: Which production constraints were absent from the demo but will dominate live use?
What hidden human steps, curated inputs, or reset conditions made the demonstration look smoother?
If this went live tomorrow, what would break first: cost, latency, staffing, or trust?
demo-fidelity-gap.body: The demo fidelity gap is where optimism borrows credibility from a prototype and hopes the operations team will settle the debt quietly later.
demo-fidelity-gap.example: "The demo answered in six seconds, never hit a timeout, and was somehow never asked the difficult cases. The fidelity gap was not subtle; it merely had a good soundtrack."
demo-fidelity-gap.misuse: Not every gap is fraud. Some are ordinary prototype limitations. The phrase becomes useful when the demo quietly omits the real cost, staffing, latency, failure handling, or data quality burden required for the promised workflow to exist outside the stage.
demo-fidelity-gap.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means hand-picked inputs, reset state, hidden human intervention, forgiving latency, omitted edge cases, and a later production plan that discovers governance, tooling, and bad customer behaviour all have speaking parts.

translations.label: executive meaning
translations.text: The demo implied a future that production has not yet agreed to finance.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A polished prototype outrunning the workflow discipline required to make it real.

demo-fidelity-gap.whyExists: Demos need curation. Production needs stamina. The term exists because people keep confusing the first for evidence of the second, especially when the prototype is elegant, the narration is confident, and the operational details have been kindly removed from view.

askNext: What exact meaning of Developer workflow is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
developer-workflow.body: Developer workflow belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.
developer-workflow.example: "The team said developer workflow as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
developer-workflow.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
developer-workflow.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

developer-workflow.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.
developer-workflow.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Windsurf need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.

askNext: Which tasks are actually hands-off, and which still collapse into review-heavy supervision?
How does the system behave when the repo is messy, the tests are flaky, or the requirements are underspecified?
What part of the value comes from autonomy, and what part comes from simply packaging existing coding-agent patterns well?
devin.body: The useful question is not whether the agent can code. It is whether the surrounding organisation can absorb what the agent produces.
devin.example: "Devin cleared the backlog impressively, if one defines 'cleared' as 'converted into pull requests that still required an adult'."
devin.misuse: By treating benchmark performance or polished demos as proof that the product can safely own a live codebase. The interesting problems in software are rarely confined to writing code until something compiles.
devin.practicalMeaning: In practice Devin means delegated development tasks, long-running tool loops, browser and terminal actions, and a new management job involving repo hygiene, task framing, review, and deciding when the agent is confidently wrong.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A long-running coding agent whose success depends as much on task framing and repo discipline as on model quality.

devin.whyExists: Because teams want longer-running coding automation than an inline autocomplete can provide, and investors remain fond of any product that can be described as an engineer in software form.

askNext: Do we need an integrated NVIDIA system, or just access to equivalent compute?
Is this for training, inference, experimentation, or institutional theatre?
What operational burden are we buying along with the speed and convenience?
dgx.body: DGX is what happens when the GPU story becomes an appliance story. It is not just more chips. It is a deliberate attempt to make the surrounding system part of the sale.
dgx.example: "The announcement said the company was investing in frontier capability. The facilities team heard 'DGX' and immediately started asking about power, airflow, and delivery dates."
dgx.misuse: By using DGX as shorthand for any powerful GPU server, or by treating the purchase of one as proof that a company has an AI strategy rather than a newly expensive room.
dgx.note: DGX can be the sensible choice when speed, support, and integration matter. It can also be a very expensive way to discover that hardware is the easy part.
dgx.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means dense, vendor-shaped compute for organisations willing to pay for an integrated system, faster deployment, and closer alignment with NVIDIA's view of how serious AI infrastructure should look.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A bundled way to acquire serious AI compute without pretending integration work is free.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: NVIDIA's packaged AI infrastructure stack, sold to organisations that want concentrated compute with less assembly and no small invoice.

dgx.whyExists: Because many teams do not want to assemble their own AI compute stack from scratch, and NVIDIA would understandably prefer to sell the whole temple rather than only the candles.

askNext: What exact meaning of DGX Cloud is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
dgx-cloud.body: DGX Cloud earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
dgx-cloud.example: "DGX Cloud came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
dgx-cloud.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
dgx-cloud.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

dgx-cloud.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
dgx-cloud.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around DGX often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: What exact tasks can this system complete without human rescue?
Where does accountability sit when the workflow makes a bad decision?
Are we buying durable automation or just renting an employee metaphor?
digital-worker.body: The phrase is useful mostly as a diagnostic. When the language turns employment-shaped, someone is usually trying to smuggle software limits past a budget committee.
digital-worker.example: "The demo promised a digital worker for claims processing. The implementation delivered an approval queue with a more theatrical org chart."
digital-worker.misuse: By speaking as if software has judgement, accountability, or broad role competence simply because it can complete a workflow. Calling it a worker does not make it adaptable, trustworthy, or employable in the human sense.
digital-worker.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a mix of rules, integrations, model calls, approval steps, and exception handling wrapped in labour metaphors so the budget can be compared to a salary instead of a software line item.

translations.label: sales meaning
translations.text: A software budget item reframed as operational staffing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An automation stack with model-assisted steps and a carefully managed definition of success.

digital-worker.warningLabel: If the product is introduced as a colleague, ask who will be blamed when it behaves like software.
digital-worker.whyExists: Because buyers understand roles more quickly than architectures, and vendors would rather sell "an accounts payable worker" than "a model-mediated process layer with brittle integrations".

askNext: What behaviour was the student model trained to copy?
Which capabilities held up after distillation, and which quietly fell off?
Did the process reduce cost without merely packaging the same errors more efficiently?
distillation.body: The smaller model may be faster, cheaper, and easier to deploy. It is still carrying somebody else's worldview in a lighter suitcase.
distillation.example: "Their distilled model was impressively cheap, which was another way of saying they had purchased fewer surprises per request."
distillation.misuse: It is misused when people describe the resulting model as independent genius rather than compressed imitation. Distillation can preserve capability surprisingly well, but it also preserves blind spots, style, and hidden assumptions.
distillation.note: Distillation is often described as model compression, but the interesting part is not just size. It is which behaviours survive the squeeze.
distillation.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means generating a large synthetic training set from a stronger model, fine-tuning a cheaper one on that data, and accepting a careful trade-off between cost, latency, and capability.

translations.label: exec meaning
translations.text: Keep most of the magic, lose most of the bill.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Training a smaller model to mimic a stronger one closely enough to be cheaper and still useful.

distillation.whyExists: It exists because frontier-grade behaviour is expensive to serve everywhere. If a smaller system can absorb enough of a stronger model's behaviour, products gain speed, lower inference cost, and a better shot at running locally or at scale.
